subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: 25 house cards, 24 outpost cards, 25 cave cards, 9 key house cards, 6 star house cards, 4 starting house cards, 1 reputation board, 4 player boards, 1 encounter book, 7 dice, 1 first-player card, 1 round marker, 4 cubes (one in each player color), 12 starting villagers, 18 villagers, 6 special villagers, 81 goods tokens (12 fruit/fish/mushroom, 10 pots/rope/paper, 8 ore, 7 amethyst), 10 cider tokens, 20 potion tokens, and coin tokens (36 ones, 6 fives, 8 tens).

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: 1) Put the reputation board in the center. 2) Place the round marker on the top chamber in the row of seven caves on the reputation board. 3) Place coin, goods, cider, and potion tokens beside the board as a shared supply. 4) Put one cider token on the reputation board's cider space. 5) Place the 6 star house cards face up in one row. 6) Shuffle key house cards, reveal 4 face up in a row below the star houses, and return the remaining key houses to the box. 7) Put special villagers aside; place remaining standard villagers face down, draw 5, and place those 5 face up across the top of the reputation board. 8) Shuffle cave cards and place them in a face-up pile next to the reputation board. 9) Shuffle house cards and outpost cards into separate face-down decks, then reveal 4 from each deck into separate face-up market rows. 10) Place encounter book and dice within reach of all players.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player gets 1 player board, 7 coins, 1 starting house card, and 3 starting villagers (one of each starting villager type shown in setup icon 1A), then places those villagers in the ready area (left grassy area). Each player takes the cube matching the banner color on their board and places it on the torch space of the reputation board. Four-player exception: the two starting villagers without hammer symbols begin exhausted (moon area), so each player only starts round 1 with one ready villager; those two exhausted starting villagers move to ready at end of round 1.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: Choose a first player and give them the first-player card. Turn order proceeds clockwise. Setup balancing coins: in 2-player, second player gets +1 coin; in 3-player, second and third each get +1 coin; in 4-player, fourth gets +1 coin. After setup, begin round 1. Each round starts with action turns beginning from the first player and proceeding clockwise until everyone passes.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: Above and Below is played over 7 rounds. Each round follows this structure: 1) Action Phase: starting with the first-player card and moving clockwise, each player takes one turn at a time, choosing exactly one normal action (Explore, Build, Harvest, Train, or Labor) or passing. Players continue cycling in turn order until all players have passed. Some players may take more turns than others. 2) End Round Phase: after all pass, resolve end-of-round steps in order: spend potions/cider if desired, rest villagers using beds, collect income, and perform round-advance upkeep from the reputation board. 3) Next Round: begin another action phase unless round 7 is complete; after round 7, proceed to final scoring.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
On your turn, you may take free actions first, then exactly one normal action. After taking a normal action, you may not take more free actions this turn.

Normal Actions:
1) Explore: Draw the top cave card to the left of your board. Move at least 2 ready villagers onto that cave card (you may send more than 2). Roll one die per exploring villager and place each die on its villager as rolled. Lantern generation per villager: roll 1+ gives 1 lantern; roll 3+ gives 2 lanterns (max 2 from a die). Use cave card result to locate the encounter paragraph in the encounter book. The player to your left reads the full paragraph, both choice labels, and explore thresholds (but not rewards in parentheses). You choose one option aloud, then must meet one of that option's explore numbers. You may Exert explorers for +1 lantern each by moving them from cave card to injured area. If successful, gain reward at highest threshold reached for your chosen option, then move remaining exploring villagers to exhausted and place the cave card to the right side of your board (in your cave/outpost row). If failed, gain no cave and no rewards, move remaining explorers to exhausted, apply any listed failure penalty, and place cave card at bottom of cave deck.
2) Build: Exhaust one ready villager with a hammer symbol. Pay coin cost to buy one available card: house, key house, star house, or outpost. Houses/key/star houses go in your house row next to starting house. Outposts go in outpost row beneath houses and must be placed on top of an open cave card you own from a prior Explore action. After building a house or outpost, refill that market row to 4 face-up cards.
3) Harvest: Exhaust one or more ready villagers. For each villager exhausted this way, take one good from one of your houses/outposts. Put that good beside your coins, place it for sale (top-left sale circle), or place it into your advancement track.
4) Train: Exhaust one ready villager with a quill symbol. Pay the listed coin cost for one available villager from the reputation board row and place that trained villager in your exhausted area. Do not refill the villager row immediately (refill occurs at end-of-round upkeep).
5) Labor: Exhaust one or more ready villagers. Gain 1 coin per villager exhausted. The first player to take Labor in a round also takes the cider token from the reputation board (if available). If no cider remains there, gain only coins.

Free Actions (before your normal action):
- Buy from another player: on your turn, you may attempt to buy one good/cider/potion that another player has placed for sale. Price is negotiated in coins but must be at least 3 coins; seller may refuse any offer. If accepted, pay seller and take item.
- Put something for sale: place one good, cider, or potion in your sale circle. You may have only one item for sale at a time.
- Remove or replace your for-sale item: only on your turn.
- Refresh building row: pay 1 coin once per turn to refresh either all 4 house cards or all 4 outpost cards in one row (send old row to bottom of that deck, reveal 4 new cards). Key/star house rows cannot be refreshed.
- Pass: declare "I pass" to leave the round. You may not take further actions or free actions this round. If you start your turn with no ready villagers, you must pass (after optional free actions).

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content: The encounter system is a branching push-your-luck mechanism. During Explore, you commit workers first, then hear a story branch and choose one of two approaches. Each approach has multiple explore thresholds and hidden rewards. Dice produce lanterns in a stepped way (1 or 2), and exerting villagers trades long-term tempo (injuries) for short-term encounter success. Village state is spatial: ready/exhausted/injured areas control worker availability and recovery speed; beds on your built cards determine how quickly villagers recover each round. Outpost expansion is gated by successful cave discovery: you cannot place an outpost unless you have an unoccupied cave card.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content: The game ends after round 7. Final scoring is based on village-point generation from your built tableau and progression systems. From the provided rule text, village points come from: (1) house/key house/star house/outpost cards that provide village points and point effects, and (2) advancement track placements, where each advancement slot grants village points and may increase income as shown on the player board. Reputation-board progress and card abilities may also contribute according to their printed symbols/effects. Player with the highest final village-point total wins. Tie-breaker is determined by the official full rulebook scoring section (not included in the provided excerpt).

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: 1) Stabilize your action economy first: make sure you can reliably perform Build and Train early (hammer + quill access matters). 2) Prioritize one early engine card that either produces a good you can repeatedly harvest or increases your economic flexibility. 3) Explore with intent, not greed: send enough villagers to hit realistic encounter thresholds, but avoid excessive exertion in rounds 1-2 unless the reward is a major tempo swing. 4) Use Labor opportunistically to claim first-cider in rounds where your other actions are blocked; cider helps recovery and can effectively add future actions.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content: 1) Over-exerting villagers in early explores. Injuries snowball into fewer available actions in future rounds. 2) Ignoring outpost prerequisites. Buying/aiming for outposts without open cave cards wastes planning and turns. 3) Training too late. New villagers enter exhausted, so delaying training reduces their lifetime contribution across only 7 rounds. 4) Treating Labor as default every turn. Labor is safe but low ceiling; overusing it can leave your village underdeveloped in points and production. 5) Forgetting free actions before normal action. Missing a timely refresh or sale listing can lock you out of better card purchases that turn.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content: 1) Explore risk vs workforce health: deciding when to exert for immediate rewards versus preserving villagers for upcoming turns. 2) Build now vs Train now: immediate point/economy card purchases compete with long-term action-capacity growth. 3) Goods allocation: keep goods for internal advancement and engines, or sell/trade for coins to hit critical buy windows in the market rows.

metadata.notes: Guide written from the provided Above and Below rulebook excerpt. The excerpt is truncated near end-round/final-scoring pages, so tie-break wording and complete point-source enumeration should be verified against the full official rulebook text.
